speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning of tape number three with Mr. Fred Findling. You were speaking to us about going to Belgium with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My mother and my-- and Regina, she was only 18 months old, stayed behind in Cologne to at least take care of personal matters and sell the-- whatever family furniture we had. But when we arrived, we were immediately after a couple of days sent to a home in northern Belgium by the sea called Ostend, O-S-T-E-N-D. And we stayed there for approximately three months. That is Joe, Fannie, Martin, and I. And then after three months, Martin and I were shipped to a boys' home in Brussels. Joe went to a foster home in Brussels and Fanny went also in another home in Brussels. Later on, after a few months, Joe joined us in the boys' home and-- but Fannie stayed in this home until my mother finally came into Belgium. Now, she arrived about six months later. And at the time that she arrived, they had already closed up the borders, and that is Belgium and Holland and the other countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had closed up the borders because they were inundated by Jews leaving Germany. So they just couldn't handle any more Jewish immigrants. And in order for my mother to get in, my cousin's husband, Kirschenstein-- no, I'm sorry, Rembelinsky. It was Rembelinsky. Should I spell it? R-E-M-B-E-L-I-N-S-K-Y, Rembelinsky. There was Herman and Jack. Herman and Jack were brothers. They survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Herman Rembelinsky who told me after the war that he paid $1,500 to a German smuggler to smuggle my mother and my sister into Belgium from Cologne, Germany. And $1,500 in those days was a lot of money. And he used the words dollars. Anyways, I didn't know that they had been in Belgium or were in Belgium. And we had a-- a sort of custom. When we walked in the streets, we would take walks every few days or once a week, and as was the custom in those days when you walked in the streets from a home, you would hold somebody's hand. And we walked in the streets of Belgium near the center of the city, when one day I saw my mother. I didn't even know she was in Belgium. And that's how we get reunited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belgium. She was staying somewhere in Belgium. She didn't know where we were. And that's how we got reunited. We discovered each other. Anyways, was after she-- after a while, she took Fannie and Reggie with her to live with her and that's where they stayed during the war. Joe, Martin, and I were in this boys' home and we stayed there until Germany invaded Belgium. That was in May, 1940. The history books will tell you probably 9th or 10th of-- 10th of May, 1940, when the Germans were at the outskirts of Brussels. The leader of our home decided it was time to high tail it out of Brussels and he joined up with the director or principal of a girls' home, Jewish home. And all of us together, about 100 children, boarded a freight train. And we boarded the last three freight cars. And that train left at 11 o'clock at night while the Germans were already at the airport. And we were able to get out of Brussels the night before the Germans came into Brussels. And our journey on this freight train without food or water took us three days before we wound up in southern France. And every once in a while, the train would stop somewhere in the country and peasants and farmers and neighboring people would come to the train and bring food and water for the people. That's how we were able to survive. The only thing we had for bedding was straw. We slept on the straw. And there were at least 30, 40 people in each box car for-- for three days. And while we was zigzagging around France trying to locate a place where we could safely end up, we also went through Amiens, which is not far from Paris, and I remember this from what people have told me. And in the middle of the night, we watched the bombardment-- the German bombardment of Paris. And I still have vivid recollections of waking up and seeing-- and seeing-- seeing air balloons that the French used to stop the dive bombers and saw flashes of light from the fire that-- both from the planes coming down and the flashes of light from the anti-aircraft guns shooting up at the planes. And for a nine-year-old boy, it was quite a war experience to see the actual fighting while it was going on. And this was also-- the train had stopped because it couldn't go any further while all the bombardment was going on. But I remember that was Amiens. And then from then we continued on and after three days, we wound up in the-- in the south, near-- not far from Toulouse. Tolouse is T-O-U-L-O-U-S-E. That's in southern France. It's a large city in southern France. And about 35 miles south of there is a city called Villefranche. Villefranche is V-I-L-L-E-F-R-A-N-C-H-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very common name in France. Almost every province has a Villefranche. It's like using Main Street. But-- so you have to identify the Villefranche. This was Villefranche de Haute-Garonne. Shall I spell it? H-A-U-T-E-G-A-R-O-N-N-E. And not far, within a few miles of Villefranche, was a little tiny village call Seyre, S-E-Y-R-E. And of course, the only way to identify that is to identify the neighboring town, which is Nailloux, N-A-I-L-L-O-U-X. So we used to say Seyre et Nailloux.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a little tiny village and this is where we wound up. The train went as far as Villefranche, and we loaded up onto-- onto trucks that were given to us and we went to Seyre et Nailloux during the-- during the fighting. France had not yet been divided. Germany was in the process of conquering France. As history will say, will attest to the fact that France was divided into the German occupied zone and the Vichy government by-- headed by Marshal Petain, who later turned out to be considered a traitor. Anyway, southern France was occupied by the French but the police were puppets and they were-- they were actually doing the bidding of the Germans. Now, at that particular time also, when we landed there, there-- there was no place for us to stay. They had 100 children and a few adults that were in charge of us. And we had no food, we had no blankets, we had nothing. So they gave us little granary that was in the village to use for-- for sleeping purposes. And they put straw on the floor, so we all slept on straw, called paille in French. And we lived there until finally, the Swiss Red Cross started sending supplies. And they sent beds, cots, blankets, and whatever they could to assist the-- the people that were homeless there. That time was also the time when the French army were being demobilized. They were all sent south after the Germans conquered France and they all were in, in villages, cities, and towns of southern France sitting around, waiting to be demobilize.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, there was a lot of ill feeling toward the Germans. And we were told if anyone spoke a word of German in the presence of the French soldiers, we would-- we'd be put on bread and water for eight days. Unfortunately, my French was not so good because I'd only lived in Belgium for a year and a half. And I spoke mostly German and my French improved while I was in southern France. But I got caught speaking a German word or saying something in German and I was put on bread and water for eight days. That was my-- my first experience of the harsh life in southern France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I say bread and water for eight days, I mean two slices of bread a day. That's all I had to eat. And of course, we didn't have much to eat anyways because most of us were scrounging around in the-- the neighborhood, the farm area, and the farmers were too kind to us. If they saw us going on one of their fig trees or apple trees, they'd shoot at you. They didn't want you there. But I became quite adept at being able to forage for food. And I could smell where a potato was being grown. I could dig up a potato-- a raw potato, brush it off with my hand, eat it. And I could do the same with onions. I knew when the onions were. So just foraging around and scrounging around helped you being able to survive. There was very little food that was available in the beginning. We just did as best we could. And I do have a recollection of going to the blacksmith one day in the village. And the blacksmith had made himself some toast and I guess he blackened it too much and discarded it. And it was totally black. And I was so starved that I ate that. And I remember eating it and feeling satisfied. But we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we back up a minute? Can you tell me some of the people, the leadership people, that took you there? Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Frank, Alex Frank. He was only 28 years old. He was the head of the group. He was a communist, a Belgian communist. But he was in charge of the boys' home and he survived the war and he's still alive today in Germany. And he took charge of the group and he probably was the oldest adult. The-- some of the people in-- in our group were teenagers or already-- 13, 14, 15, 16-year-olds. And of course, they helped out and of course, there were some as young as three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that the age group of all the children varied and the older ones took on more responsibility in keeping the group together. And they-- they would go and work in the fields for farmers for-- in exchange for food. They would find any work that was available where they could get some benefits from that to help us survive. You were saying in the granary at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: That granary we turned into a home. After the beds came and the blankets came, then the whole place was changed. We-- one of the rooms we use for a dining room, another one we used for-- a big hall, we used for a bedroom. Of course, when we talk about the cots, the cots were put right next to each other so there was no room in between. And in order to get everybody to sleep, you would-- some people would have to sleep between the cots and it became kind of difficult sometimes to start sleeping there. And usually what-- what happened after you fell asleep, you fell into the middle of the bed and two of you slept on the cot. But it was made so that there-- there would be room for people to sleep right between the cot and you would have to sleep there. And of course, in the wintertime, you didn't have any heat so the blankets is all you had. Another thing that I wanted to bring to your attention is that we didn't have any shoes. So we were given wooden shoes called sabot, S-A-B-O-T. And we wore sabot and we became quite good at them in southern France. And when you first got them, they gave you a little insert, cloth insert. But they became impractical because they got full of ice and snow and you wind up just putting your feet into the sabot without socks and walking in the middle of the winter with those and with ice and water forming in them. But you-- as a child, you learned how-- how to maneuver on those and that's what we had as shoes in southern France. We didn't have any clothing. We had shorts, we didn't have any long pants. And in terms of warm clothing, they started knitting their own sweaters. And I learned how to knit. In fact, there's a picture of me somewhere where it shows a sweater that was knit with my help also that I wore in the winter of 1940-41 in Seyre et Nailloux. It was a-- we didn't starve to death. We didn't have any food, but we were able to survive. And we were undernourished, there's no question about that. And we didn't have much, if anything. I do have one recollection, a vivid recollection. For breakfast, we usually got a little bit of milk and that's all you got. No seconds. And if something happened to that, that's it. And I remember one day, I accidentally spilled the milk and there was just a little bit left in the cup. And I remembered how cats drank their milk and I put my tongue to that and that little bit that remained in the cup and made that do. I just-- touching the tip of my tongue to that milk. And it actually filled me up to-- to be able to do that. I was proud of myself that I could-- that I had the discipline to learn how to deal with adversity. But anyways, back-- now going back to Seyre et Nailloux, we lived there for about a year. And during that time, conditions got more severe. The Germans started making more demands, the-- the French police started reporting that the-- the Jews were not welcome. And then all of a sudden, the-- the announcement came, any Jew 16 years old and older had to report to the police station. And we all knew what that meant. I mean, it meant you're going to be deported, probably sent to some camp. So of course, arrangements were made immediately to try and go into hiding. And the older ones left and went south into the Pyrenees near Foix, F-O-I-X, looking for a place. And they found an old abandoned castle, 15th-16th century castle, that was called Castle of the hill. The French were is Chateau de la Hille. Chateau de la Hille is spelled Chateau and hille is spelled H-I-L-L-E. And arrangements were made to take the whole group into the chateau eventually because that would be perhaps the best place to hide from the prying eyes of the French police and the Germans. And six months before I came to United States in-- sometime in the winter of 1940-41, probably early 1941, we all boarded trucks and we went south to Chateau le Hille. And the people-- the advance group that went there, the older boys and older girls, had gone there and put in the electricity. And we didn't have any toilets, so they put electricity and water. And for toilets, they dug a big hole at the back of the chateau, put wooden boards around it, and that became our toilet-- just a big hole with boards over it-- until they eventually were able to get plumbing in and get toilets. But for a few months, that's all we had. And when we went there--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there from Seyre by trucks. We hired-- somewhere, we got a hold of some trucks and we went south. And all of us came to Chateau le Hille. And from there is where I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the life in this chateau.

speaker: interviewee
text: The chateau-- we were completely unknown. We-- the older people would go to the next village and work if they could. We planted our own food, we had our own garden. We were pretty well self-sufficient. And it was really operated almost like a farm. We would have-- we would go and get goat's milk from the neighbors. I think we had-- we had-- I think we had some goats that we were able to get for the young children. And we had the life of a kibbutz. It was completely self-sufficient. And the neighboring farms used the older people's help and probably gave them in the barter system food back for whatever we needed. But that's how we survived as a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were in danger there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not ever in the danger because this was still-- this was already in the foothills of the Pyrenees Mountains. And we knew that there was trouble brewing, but we did not know at that particular time that it was an imminent danger. Our people were making arrangements already at that time to get out. And at that particular time, Eleanor Roosevelt started the program of Save the Jews-- Save the Children. And the American Quakers opened up eight offices in Europe to try and assist any Jewish child who could get parental permission to help them get to the United States as part of this Save the Children program. And my brother, Joe, still knew the whereabouts of my mother, wrote to her. And she gave written permission for us to go. And based upon that letter that my mother wrote giving her permission, the American Quakers made the arrangements for us to travel to the United States. And In June of 1941, Joe, Martin, and I went to Marseilles and in Marseilles the American Quakers had their organization. And from there, after six weeks staying in Marseilles, we were given travel documents to travel by train through Spain into Lisbon, Portugal to catch a boat from Lisbon, Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: You traveled on your own with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- we were joined with children from other areas from France that also had gotten parental permission. In my group from Chateau de la Hille, there were I'm guessing now maybe 10 or 12, not all of them, that were given parental permission to travel and were part of that group. We were the second group. There had been one group that preceded us that went to the United States. We were the second group and the last group to get out under Eleanor Roosevelt's program before the United States entered the war and of course, then the rescue the children program completely ceased. But from Marseilles, we-- we were able to board a train, all of us, and there were about 50 some in the group from various parts of Europe. And we went all the way to Lisbon through Spain. And Lisbon, we stayed there four in a villa for six weeks until finally we caught a boat. We were supposed to go on a boat first to Cuba, called the N-Y-A-S-S-A. But for some reason, we never got that boat and we finally got a boat, a Portuguese boat, that used to be owned by Britain called S-E-R-P-A, Pinto, P-I-N-T-O. And that boat went from Lisbon to-- to Casablanca, made a stop in Casablanca, then from Casablanca went to Bermuda and finally from Bermuda went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 days. And it was a-- it was a freight boat. And of course, we had steerage, you know. It was pretty uncomfortable. But who cared? We were on the way to America. And we had a couple of alarms, alerts of German torpedoes, torpedo boats being in the area. But these things were just false alarms and we were able to make it. The-- the boat took a slow way and zigzagged in order to avoid the German U-boats and finally landed in New York on September 24, 1941. And we saw the Statue of Liberty and we all knew we were safe. Now, my mother and my sisters, going back to 1940, in May 1940, when Germany overran Belgium, they were trapped. They didn't get out. And they stayed behind in Germany-- in-- in Brussels. And in 1943, they had to go into hiding because the Germans were rounding up all the Jews from Brussels. There were 25,000 Jews that were rounded up from Belgium. And my mother evidently being in hiding in the attic got stir crazy. And as my cousins tell me, she thought she could pass by for a Belgian. And she left my two sisters in the attic and went into the street and was picked up. And the last one to saw her was my first cousin in Belgium, my mother's older sister's son, Joe Kirchenstein, K-I-R-C-H-E-N-S-T-E-I-N, who also survived the war. He had been picked up by the Germans and he was taken to a collecting point in Belgium. From there, they went-- from there, they were shipped to Auschwitz. He's the last one that saw my mother there. It's called Maligne, M-A-L-I-G-N-E, where he was. And he talked to my mother on the other side of the fence. And he even knew what transport she went and when she was taken by transport directly to Auschwitz and immediately gassed and cremated on August 1st, 1943. The same day they arrived, she was cremated. The reason I know that is because the people that were made into slave laborers, they put a tattoo on them. If they didn't get the tattoo, that meant you went right to the gas chamber and that's how she perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My sisters, immediately when that happened, they put them in-- they were able to place them in a Catholic home, in a convent. And they were hid by the-- the nuns in a Catholic convent for the rest of the war. And after the war, of course, Fannie, my oldest sister, wanted to become a nun. She already started taking her vows. And she was she became a die hard Catholic convert. And what happened is that after the war, my cousins had difficulty getting them-- getting them out of the convent even for visitation. So one weekend, they spirited my sisters out of there and while they took them out, they shipped them to London to my cousin-- to my uncles, Herman [? Gottesdien ?] and Charles Gordon. And while in London, it took them six weeks to deprogram her. He said when he started talking to her, she would spit fire and brimstone at him. Christ is going to punish you and all the Catholic dogma. And she really wanted to become a Catholic. But after six weeks, she finally realized that she's better off going back to her roots. So then when she came back, they wouldn't give her back to the Catholic-- to the Catholic convent. And the Catholic convent started a lawsuit against my cousin, saying that they had a prior claim to my sisters. And they said the only one that could claim her was my mother, who was not around. They said that my cousins didn't have a prior claim. But before the issue could be resolved, my sisters were able to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they brought here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American Jewish organization part of the-- I forgot the exact name, but it was part of a Jewish program to bring and reunite families of displaced people who had survived the war. And my two sisters came here in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- they survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, as far as our living in southern France, Chateau la Hille, the reason I think it is so interesting is because when we left, we thought that the rest of the people perished because the Germans overran southern France and we know what happened to the Jews. There must have been in total about 100 people and we knew of no survivors after the war. They just-- when I came back to visit Chateau de la Hille as a young man, nobody knew what happened to any of them. They just had disappeared. It now turns out that Max Frank was alive, went to Germany after the war, and worked eventually in eastern Germany. That's why he didn't have contact. He was in the eastern part of Germany and until the German reunification, he had very little communication with the West. Finally, after Germany was reunified, he started to look for his family because he considered all of the children of Chateau de la Hille part of his family. And he was able to discover that most of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had escaped. And they had a reunion in Los Angeles several years ago. And in that reunion, they-- they found that there were three missing people, the Findling brothers. Nobody knew where they were. And one day, my sister, who teaches in San Francisco, met a-- one of the congregation members and said, are you somehow related to the Findling brothers? That was your reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here. This is the end of tape number three.
